channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: DeepSeek’s Sequel Set to Extend China’s Reach in Open-Source A.I.
item.description: Chinese companies have embraced making their most advanced artificial intelligence models available to all.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Chinese start-up DeepSeek shook the industry in January 2025 with its claim that it had created an advanced A.I. system for a fraction of what its American rivals spent.

item.title: War and Sanctions Accelerate China’s Currency Push
item.description: China’s bid to build a renminbi-based financial system beyond the U.S. dollar’s reach is gaining traction as a way to sidestep sanctions.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Krolik

item.title: Pentagon Fires Stars and Stripes Newspaper’s Ombudsman
item.description: The newspaper’s ombudsman, Jacqueline Smith, said she had been given no reason for her dismissal.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Valerie Plesch for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Pentagon has been taking steps to increase its editorial control of Stars and Stripes, which has covered the military continuously since World War II.

item.title: Soldier Used Classified Information to Bet on Maduro’s Ouster, U.S. Says
item.description: Federal prosecutors say that Sgt. Gannon Ken Van Dyke, who was involved in the operation to oust Nicolás Maduro from power in Venezuela, used the information to place bets on a prediction market.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser and Jonah E. Bromwich
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Nicolás Maduro, former President of Venezuela, being escorted off a helicopter to be taken to the federal courthouse in Manhattan in January.

item.title: Nike to Cut 1,400 Jobs as Part of Its Turnaround Plan
item.description: Most of the layoffs will affect tech workers as the athletic giant tries to reverse a yearslong sales slump. It’s the second round of cuts this year.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Gabby Jones for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nike said on Thursday that layoffs would affect workers across North America, Europe and Asia.

item.title: Microsoft Targets About 7% of Its U.S. Workers With Buyout Offer
item.description: The tech giant is offering long-serving employees early retirements as it continues to invest aggressively in artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Karen Weise
item.media:credit: Grant Hindsley for The New York Times
item.media:description: Microsoft and other big tech companies are on a spending spree to build the infrastructure for artificial intelligence.

item.title: Two Litigation Partners Depart Paul Weiss
item.description: Kannon Shanmugam and Masha Hansford are the latest in a string of litigators who have left the influential New York law firm.
item.dc:creator: Jessica Silver-Greenberg and Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:description: The Paul Weiss partners Kannon Shanmugam and Masha Hansford are departing to start a practice focusing on Supreme Court and other appellate litigation at the rival firm Davis Polk.

item.title: New Gene Therapy Enables Children With a Rare Form of Deafness to Hear
item.description: The treatment, the first of its kind, was approved by the Food and Drug Administration on Thursday. “Our baby was born deaf, and now he can hear,” said one parent.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: Miles was born with otoferlin deafness, a rare form of deafness caused by a mutation in a single gene. But with a new gene therapy, he can hear.

item.title: Congress May Finally Recognize That Mobile Homes Aren’t Really That Mobile
item.description: An obscure federal rule requires manufactured homes to be built on a chassis, making them more costly. A bill in Congress would remove the mandate, enacted five decades ago.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Goldstein
item.media:credit: Carlos Bernate for The New York Times
item.media:description: Manufactured homes being installed in Petersburg, Va. A 1970s law requires all manufactured homes to have a steel chassis, a rule from the days when these structures were called mobile homes.

item.title: How We Cover the White House Correspondents’ Dinner
item.description: The annual black-tie event attracts Hollywood stars, reporters and politicians for an irreverent night celebrating press freedom.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Politicians, Hollywood stars and journalists representing more than 100 news organizations attend the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner in Washington. Above, the scene in 2024.

item.title: Warner Bros. Discovery Shareholders Approve Paramount Deal
item.description: The vote brings the tech scion David Ellison one step closer to uniting some of the world’s biggest news and entertainment properties.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Warner Bros. Studio in Burbank, Calif. The vote on Thursday vote does not end the wrangling over the deal Paramount’s deal for Warner Bros.

item.title: Investors Fret Over Tesla’s $25 Billion A.I. Bet
item.description: Elon Musk’s electric vehicle company said that it needs to spend big on artificial intelligence and robots. Wall Street is worried about the bill.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Michelle Leder and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:description: Tesla’s giant bet on artificial intelligence, humanoid robots and more isn’t going over well with investors this morning.

item.title: Why Diesel Has Become a Much Bigger Economic Problem Than Gasoline
item.description: The war in Iran has disrupted supplies of diesel, used to power trucks and heavy equipment, much more than gasoline, which is primarily used in passenger cars.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Adam Amengual for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Port of Los Angeles. Large semi trucks and some ships run on diesel fuel.

item.title: As U.S. Brands Stumble, China Wins Over Young Indonesians
item.description: A new generation of Indonesian consumers view Chinese brands as high-tech and of good quality, a departure from the days when “made in China” was associated with cheap goods.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Hasya Nindita
item.media:credit: Ulet Ifansasti for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Glodok market in the Chinatown area of Jakarta, Indonesia’s capital, decked out for Lunar New Year in February.

item.title: Controversies and Production Issues Around ‘Michael,’ the Jackson Biopic, Explained
item.description: Reshoots, reluctant studios and family holdouts: the production faced many issues. But now the box office is expected to be huge.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:description: Jaafar Jackson as Michael Jackson, his uncle, in the new biopic “Michael.”

item.title: Some Labubu Dolls Contain Cotton Banned By Forced Labor Law, Testing Shows
item.description: A test verified by The New York Times found that clothes for some of the viral dolls contained cotton from the Xinjiang region of China, which is banned in the United States.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson, Sapna Maheshwari and Meaghan Tobin
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Jared Soares for The New York Times
item.media:description: For businesses like Pop Mart, selling products that contain cotton banned in the United States could have severe consequences.

item.title: Oil Prices Rise and Stocks Slide Amid Uncertainty Over Next Steps in War
item.description: Investors wrestled with uncertainty about the cease-fire between the United States and Iran.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Heidi O’Neill, Formerly of Nike, Will Be New Lululemon’s New CEO
item.description: Heidi O’Neill will join the maker of yoga pants and other athletic clothing in September.
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:description: Heidi O’Neill will join Lululemon at a challenging time for the company, which has seen its North American sales decline amid intense competition.

item.title: Tesla Profit Rises but Remains Below Earlier Highs
item.description: The company is investing in robots and self-driving taxis that are not yet generating significant sales.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:credit: Ariana Gomez for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Tesla Robotaxi in Austin, Texas, last year. The self-driving vehicles have yet to generate significant revenue for the automaker.

item.title: Iran Again Tightens Its Grip on Shipping in the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Traffic in the strait has all but halted as Iran renews its attacks, striking two vessels on Wednesday. More than 300 ships linked to Iran have passed through the strait since the war began.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A ship sailing toward the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday.

item.title: Coinbase and Gemini Accused of Illegal Gambling in N.Y. Lawsuits
item.description: Attorney General Letitia James said the cryptocurrency giants were illegally running gambling platforms in the state after they expanded into prediction markets, which are loosely regulated.
item.dc:creator: Hurubie Meko

item.title: Former Employee Sues MrBeast’s Company, Alleging Harassment
item.description: The former employee of the YouTube star’s production company said in a federal lawsuit that she had experienced sexual harassment and pregnancy discrimination. Beast Industries denies all of the claims.
item.dc:creator: Alisha Haridasani Gupta and Madison Malone Kircher
item.media:description: A former employee of Beast Industries said the company had fostered a widespread culture of demeaning female employees.

item.title: Trump Administration Nears Loan Deal to Rescue Spirit Airlines
item.description: The Trump administration is negotiating a deal that would provide as much as $500 million to the struggling company, which is in its second bankruptcy in two years.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch and Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Spirit Airlines has not reported a profit since 2019 and is currently in its second bankruptcy.

item.title: Bessent Backs Financial Support for Oil-Rich U.A.E.
item.description: The Treasury secretary said that currency swap line would benefit both the United Arab Emirates and the United States.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent testified on Capitol Hill on Wednesday.

item.title: Crypto Entrepreneur Justin Sun Sues Trump Family’s World Liberty Financial, Accusing It of Fraud
item.description: Justin Sun, one of the largest backers of World Liberty Financial, sued the Trump family’s crypto start-up, accusing it of a fraudulent scheme to pressure him into buying digital coins.
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Katarina Premfors for The New York Times
item.media:description: Justin Sun, a crypto entrepreneur, at a conference last year in Dubai, United Arab Emirates.

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Oil Company Argument in Fight Over Great Lakes Pipeline
item.description: The justices sided with Michigan officials, who have raised environmental alarms and pushed to decommission an aging section of the pipeline.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Cydni Elledge for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Enbridge Energy pump station yard in Mackinaw City, Mich., in 2024. The aging section of pipeline at issue is part of a vast network of lines that carry crude oil and natural gas liquids through Wisconsin and Michigan, before terminating in Ontario.

item.title: Anthropic’s New Mythos A.I. Model Sets Off Global Alarms
item.description: Mythos has triggered emergency responses from central banks and intelligence agencies globally, as Anthropic decides who has access to the powerful model.
item.dc:creator: Paul Mozur and Adam Satariano

item.title: Kevin Warsh, Trump’s Fed Pick, Has Tough Task Shedding ‘Sock Puppet’ Label
item.description: Kevin M. Warsh, at his confirmation hearing to lead the Federal Reserve, sought to dispel concerns that he would cut interest rates to please President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh, the president’s nominee for Federal Reserve chair, testified on Tuesday during a hearing before the Senate Banking Committee on Capitol Hill.

item.title: The Consequences of SpaceX’s Latest A.I. Moonshot
item.description: Elon Musk’s rocket and satellite internet titan may pay $60 billion for an A.I. coding start-up. It further clouds SpaceX’s once-simple business plan.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Jessica Testa
item.media:description: SpaceX increasingly isn’t just about space anymore.

item.title: Lufthansa Cuts 20,000 Flights to Save Fuel Amid Iran War Price Surge
item.description: Global jet fuel prices have jumped more than 70 percent since the start of the war, pressuring airlines in Europe, the largest consumers of jet fuel shipped through the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Lufthansa said it would cancel short-haul flights, focusing on unprofitable routes from Frankfurt and Munich.

item.title: Stocks Rise as Investors Weigh Cease-Fire Extension
item.description: Oil prices stablized as investors scrambled to make sense of President Trump’s decision to extend the cease-fire with Iran
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Hoarding Is Driving Energy Prices Higher Everywhere
item.description: As wealthy nations scramble to secure stocks of oil, the result is higher prices for all and shortages in vulnerable countries.
item.dc:creator: Peter S. Goodman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: People queue for cooking gas cylinders in New Delhi this month.

item.title: A $440,000 Breast Reduction: How Doctors Cashed In on the No Surprises Act and Arbitration
item.description: A law meant to end surprise medical billing accidentally created a multibillion-dollar industry that is making doctors richer.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff and Margot Sanger-Katz
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki for The New York Times

item.title: Musk’s SpaceX Goals Shift Ahead of Its I.P.O.
item.description: As SpaceX prepares to go public, Mr. Musk has proposed moonshots that differ from the company’s original aim of reaching Mars.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac

item.title: Aging in Place: How Technology Might Help You Grow Old at Home
item.description: The budding field is turning dreams into reality for older adults who are eager to age in place, filling caregiving gaps and easing minds as America ages rapidly.
item.dc:creator: Susan Shain

item.title: Two Drugs Stir Hope for Treatment of Deadly Pancreatic Cancer
item.description: In separate clinical trials, two treatments showed promise that they could help patients with one of the most dire diagnoses in oncology.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins and Gina Kolata
item.media:description: An animated image showing the human body highlighting the pancreas.

item.title: Devin Nunes Departs Trump Media After 4 Years as C.E.O.
item.description: The former congressman said it was an “appropriate time” for a leadership change at Truth Social’s parent company, whose share price has floundered.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Goldstein
item.media:description: Former Representative Devin Nunes with President Trump, who chose him to run Trump Media & Technology in late 2021.

item.title: SpaceX Strikes Deal With Cursor for $60 Billion
item.description: The potential acquisition comes as Elon Musk’s rocket and satellite maker, which has been emphasizing artificial intelligence, is preparing to go public.
item.dc:creator: Erin Griffith, Mike Isaac and Ryan Mac
item.media:description: SpaceX said a deal with Cursor gave it the option to buy the artificial intelligence start-up, which makes code-writing software, for $60 billion later this year.

item.title: Gates Foundation Is Conducting an External Review of Its Epstein Ties
item.description: The action comes after the release of the Epstein files, in which Bill Gates and some former advisers figure prominently.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer
item.media:description: One of the largest philanthropies in the world, the Gates Foundation has been unsettled by the release of the Epstein files.

item.title: Labor Secretary’s Departure Gives Trump an Opening to Focus on His Agenda
item.description: President Trump’s second-term goals to foster apprenticeships and manufacturing work rely on a Labor Department that has been distracted by internal turmoil.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Davis O’Brien and Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Labor Department building in Washington. Much of President Trump’s second-term agenda is rooted at the department.

item.title: U.S. Considers Financial Support for Oil-Rich U.A.E.
item.description: President Trump acknowledged that the Gulf state has incurred significant damage during the war with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump visiting the United Arab Emirates last May.

item.title: A.I. Is Eliminating Jobs on Wall Street
item.description: “A.I. gives us places to go we haven’t gone,” said one bank’s chief executive.
item.dc:creator: Rob Copeland
item.media:description: Brian T. Moynihan, Bank of America’s chief executive, said “eliminating work and applying technology” helped the bank shed 1,000 jobs last quarter.

item.title: Strait of Hormuz May Not Return to Normal, Whether It’s Open or Closed
item.description: The energy industry is planning for a future where the choke point on Iran’s southern coast is a lot less important.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels off Musandam, Oman, on Monday. Iran will not forget how easy it is to strangle shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: A criminal inquiry into the Fed looms large over the Warsh hearing.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:description: President Trump and Senator Tim Scott arrived at the Federal Reserve’s headquarters last year and were joined by Jerome H. Powell, the Fed chair.

item.title: Trump Seeks Suitors for Spirit Airlines While Dismissing Another Merger
item.description: As he rejected the idea of a merger of United Airlines and American Airlines, President Trump said someone, including possibly the federal government, should buy or help Spirit.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump on Tuesday said he would welcome a deal that might rescue Spirit Airlines from bankruptcy.

item.title: A Year After U.S.A.I.D.’s Death, Fired Workers Find Few Jobs and Much Loss
item.description: People have plowed through savings, cashed out retirement funds and moved in with relatives. Former U.S.A.I.D. workers estimate that less than half have found full-time work.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Bumiller and Eileen Sullivan
item.category: United States Agency for International Development
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times

item.title: Kevin Warsh, Trump’s Fed Chair Pick, Faces Skepticism Over Claim of Independence
item.description: Kevin Warsh’s nomination to lead the Federal Reserve could still be delayed by a Justice Department investigation into the current chair, Jerome H. Powell.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:description: Kevin M. Warsh, President Trump’s pick to lead the Federal Reserve, appeared on Tuesday before the Senate Banking Committee on Capitol Hill.

item.title: What to Expect in Apple’s New Era
item.description: Tim Cook’s replacement as chief executive, John Ternus, knows hardware engineering. Can he be a product visionary and a global diplomat?
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Vivienne Walt
item.media:description: Apple has named John Ternus as the successor to Tim Cook.

item.title: UnitedHealth Group Reports Flat Earnings in the First Quarter
item.description: The nation’s largest insurer’s profits exceeded analysts’ expectations but still fell short of showing a significant comeback.
item.dc:creator: Reed Abelson
item.media:description: The UnitedHealth Group headquarters in Minnetonka, Minn.

item.title: Tim Cook Was Very, Very Good at Making Money
item.description: If the Steve Jobs era was defined by technological innovation, the Tim Cook period was one of exceptional financial growth.
item.dc:creator: Tripp Mickle and Karl Russell
